spec_match.rationale: Editor listens on `beforeinput`, `paste`, and `drop` and routes file items from `dataTransfer.items` into inline `` / `

interactive.rationale: Editor is live and accepts dropped images / IME stickers on a real device — but there are no on-page controls (no `simulate insertion` button, no MIME toggle). Desktop visitors without an external image to drag have nothing to interact with.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb promises desktop drag-and-drop fallback, which works. But the desktop visitor still has to leave the page to find an image — the project guidance says simulate the experience on desktop too, with a button.

edge_cases.rationale: Handles image/* and video/*, multi-item loops are present, but no rejection branch for non-media items, no `clipboardData` differentiation logging, and no `URL.revokeObjectURL` cleanup so blobs accumulate across many insertions.

design_system.rationale: Variables-only colours, no glyph icons, button overrides not applicable (no styled buttons on the page).

openQuestions.title: Add a `simulate IME insert` button for desktop
openQuestions.detail: Per the design rules, every concept must be hands-on on desktop too. Add a small toolbar with `insert GIF / PNG / WebP` buttons that fire a synthetic `beforeinput` (or use the same canvas-generated technique the sibling demos use) so a desktop visitor without an image in their clipboard still sees the path execute.

openQuestions.title: Revoke object URLs on removal
openQuestions.detail: URL.createObjectURL is called on every insertion but never revoked. Long sessions leak memory. Wrap each created URL in a small lifecycle helper that revokes on element removal.

openQuestions.title: Reject non-media items explicitly
openQuestions.detail: A pasted text item currently silently does nothing (loop runs, no branch matches). Add an else branch that logs `rejected non-media item type=...` so the developer sees the discriminator pattern.

openQuestions.title: Differentiate paste from drop from IME insert
openQuestions.detail: All three currently produce similar log lines. Tag each event in the log with its source (`paste`, `drop`, `beforeinput`) and the `event.inputType` so the difference between user clipboard, OS drag, and IME commit is visible.

summary: The simplest of the six concepts — and that simplicity is also its weakness: no on-page trigger means a desktop visitor without a handy image gets nothing. Add a simulate button and the demo becomes complete.
